0: <title>Analysis of greenhouse gas emissions from the average Irish milk production system </title>
Department of Biosystems Engineering (Bioresources Modelling Group), University College Dublin, Earlsfort Terrace, Dublin 2, Ireland
Received 8 December 2003; received in revised form 13 August 2004; accepted 10 September 2004
1: Actions to moderate the major emission contributors of enteric fermentation, fertiliser and manure management on farms should not simply move the emissions elsewhere in the system, but actually reduce them. Life cycle assessment methodology was used to provide an objective framework for estimating emissions and to evaluate emission management scenarios with respect to kg CO2 eq emitted per unit of milk produced. An average dairy unit was defined and emissions were compartmentalised to calculate a total emission of 1.50 gCOeqkg-1 (energy corrected milk) yr-1 and 1.3 with economic allocation between milk and meat. Of the total emissions, 49% was enteric fermentation, 21% fertiliser, 13% con- centrate feed, 11% dung management and 5% electricity and diesel consumption. Scenario testing indicated that more efficient COWS with extensive management could reduce emissions by 14-18%, elimination of non-milking animals could reduce emissions by 14-26% and a com- bination of both could reduce emissions by 28-33%. It was concluded that the evolution of the Irish dairy sector, driven by the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP), should result in reduced GHG emissions.
Keywords: Life cycle assessment; Greenhouse gas emissions; Dairy; Milk; Ireland

3: Quantification of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions is of scientific, social and political importance. To understand and manage reduction of GHG emissions the whole system should be considered (Phetteplace et al., 2001), not just components conventionally used for policy reporting purposes (Intergovernmental Panel on Cli- mate Change, 1996b). The focus of the work presented in this paper was to define a method that evaluated the whole system with respect to GHG emissions. It was con- sidered important not only to include the major emissions but also the amplifying or attenuating effects of secondary (or minor) emission contributors. The method was applied to Irish milk production, which is predominantly from grazed and conserved grass. A life cycle assessment (LCA) type method was adopted because it defines a reproducible, objective method of delimiting the production system, and it can be used to define and quantify GHG emissions. LCA is a holistic tool used to assess the environmental impacts of a system or product from "cradle to grave" using a range of impact categories (Udo de Haes, 1996), and is defined by an international standard (International Standards Authority, 1997, 1998). To carry out an LCA a number of impact categories ranging from acidification to energy consumption are used. LCA methodology has been used for assessing a number of agricultural sys- items. Audsley et al. (1997) and Ceuterick, 1996, 1998) compiled examples of com- plete LCAs for single crops and production processes. Kramer et al. (1999) used part of the methodology to assess GHG emissions related to Dutch crop production system. Flessa et al. (2002) similarly evaluated GHG emissions from two farming systems in southern Germany and showed the important contribution of individual gases to climate change. De Boer (2003), Cederberg and Mattsson (2000) and Haas et al. (2001) further illustrated the possibilities of using LCA to compare agricultural production systems.

For this work a complete LCA was not undertaken as only impacts associated with GHGs were considered. In general, to produce a robust LCA those factors that are considered to be very small contributers to the system are ignored. An IPCC level 1 type assessment (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 1996b) of dairy production in Ireland would suggest that about 80% of emissions might be accounted for by enteric fermentation, manure management and fertiliser. The lesser contributors, or those beyond the geo-political boundary, are ignored. This means that there are a number of system attributes, each of which may be small, but together account for ~20% of the system emissions. This paper evaluated these contributors to find out whether they amplify or attenuate the con- sequences of management actions aimed at reducing emissions from the major sources.

The objectives of the study were to: (i) define an average Irish dairy unit (not whole farm); (ii) define its system boundary; (iii) produce a system inventory; (iv) estimate the GHG emissions for the system; (v) assess GHG emissions per functional unit; (vi) assess three scenarios currently being considered to reduce Irish GHG emissions; and (vii) evaluate the role of smaller components within the system.

6: The system boundary is defined by the GHG emissions associated with Irish milk production from "cradle to farm-gate". The system (Fig. 1) includes the physical limits of the dairy unit and its activities: (i) the emissions associated with the individ- ual ingredients of the concentrate feed production, transport and processing; (ii) emissions associated with N fertiliser production, transportation and application; (iii) emissions associated with livestock and related manure management; (iv) emis- sions associated with electricity used for cooling milk, and diesel for agricultural operations (e.g fertiliser application, manure application and forage production). As in previous studies emissions associated with the production of medicines, insec- ticides, machines, buildings and roads are excluded because of lack of data (Ceder- berg and Mattsson, 2000). Direct N2O emissions from cattle were excluded from the study as these are known to be negligible (Tiedje, 1988), and CO2 from enteric fer- mentation was also excluded because it is generally regarded as recycling from susta- inably produced plant matter, and thus makes no net addition to the atmosphere (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 1993). Geo-political boundaries are not considered as limits to the system (in contrast to IPCC level 1 assessment (Inter- governmental Panel on Climate Change, 1996b)).

Where (i) is concentrate feed related, (ii) is fertiliser related, (iii) is manure management related and (iv) relates to electricity and diesel usage.

9: The functional unit (FU) is an attribute of the product or system and is used as a quantitative scalar for comparison purposes. The FU must be definable and measur- able and therefore a system may have a number of possible functions (International Standards Authority, 1997). For dairy systems the main product is milk, and SO the FU defined was: the production of 1 kg of energy corrected milk (ECM) over a time frame of one year. ECM (kg) was determined as follows: ECM = 0.25*M + 12.2*F + 7.7*P (1)

where M is the mass of milk (kg), F is the fat (kg) and P is the protein (kg) (Sjaunja et al., 1990). Irish milk has an average density of 1.03 kgL-1, , and contains on aver- age 3.9% fat and 3.2% protein (McDonagh et al., 1999). This FU encompasses the main product and the natural cycle, driven by lactation periods (Wegener Sleeswijk et al., 1996), and in Ireland is one year. As a mass or volume measure the FU is appropriate for GHG emissions because it is applicable on a global scale (Haas et al., 2000).
10: Global warming potential (GWP) was used to determine the contribution of three gases (CO2, CH4 and N20) to the greenhouse effect. The GWP index is defined as the cumulative radiative forcing effect between the present moment and a selected time in the future caused by a unit mass of gas emitted in the present. The emissions are measured in terms of a reference gas, CO2 (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 1996a). The GWP of 1 kg CO2 is 1, of 1 kg CH4 is 21, and of 1 kg N2O is 310 (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 1996a , assuming a 100-year time horizon). where Mi is the mass (in kg) of the emitted gas (Heijungs et al., 1992). The total im- pact is expressed as kg CO2 eq per functional unit.
11: <header>2.4. Data acquisition and definition of an average dairy unit </header>

In Ireland ~1.2 M dairy COWS on ~27,000 farms consume grass in the field or as conserved forage when housed, with additional feed as concentrates when needed. Weather permits 190-240 days grazing per year. The system can operate over a range of farm sizes to produce 5.55 X 109 L of milk quota (European Community, 2003). The current "ideal" system is defined by O'Donovan et al. (2000), but to define an average dairy unit data were collated from National Farm Surveys (Heavey et al., 1998; Burke and Roche, 1999, 2000, 2001; Connolly et al., 2002) for the years 1997-2001.
It was assumed that all manure (slurry) produced was land-spread at a rate of 50 m³ ha-1 The slurry stores were 75% emptied by 1st May and the remaining 25% by

1st June each year (Carton and Magette, 1999). This arrangement is for slurry stored under slats and is important because there was no storage over the summer period when emissions would be high (Husted, 1994).

Daily excreta of 0.053 m 3 d 1 (Department of Agriculture and Rural Development Northern Ireland, 2003) per cow with a N content of 0.2629 kgd 1 per cow (Smith and Frost, 2000) were adopted along with a daily CH4 EF adapted from Jarvis et al. (1995). CH4 emissions were estimated based on the area of a dung deposit (0.075 m², the number of deposits per cow per day (11) (Marsh and Campling, 1970) the CH4 emission rate, and the number of animals. An EF for dung deposited in the collecting yard before during and after milking was adapted from Misselbrook et al. (2001) by assuming a cow spends 3 h per day (O'Donovan et al., 2000) at milking and requires 1.25 m² (Department of Agriculture, Food and Rural Development, 1997). The herd in one lactation/year will produce 3.84 x 10 7 kg N20 and 2.3 X 10 kg CH4.
Fertiliser usage was taken from the Fertiliser Survey (Coulter et al., 2002). The fertiliser data were incorporated as compound used for grazing application and com- pound applied to silage ground.

Concentrate feed is used for energy supplementation in early spring and late sum- mer when grass production cannot fully support the herd, and throughout the winter period when housed. A standardised mixture of ingredients was developed after con- sultation with feed suppliers (Table 1) and feed rates were estimated by Fingleton (2003). The data used were subject to allocation (Nielson, personal communication, 2004) and SO the kg CO2 eq. contribution does not include components that could be allocated to co-products or waste. Concentrate feed was not allocated to non-milk producing stock.
EFs for CH4 and N2O per t km for trucks were adapted from Bos (1997) and the importation of fertiliser and concentrate ingredients were assumed to be in shipments

17: <header>2.5. Allocation </header>

All milk production systems generate more than one functional output, e.g. milk and meat (from culled COWS and calves). Therefore a certain percentage of the emis- sion from the dairy unit has to be allocated to the meat as well as the milk (Ceder- berg and Stadig, 2002). A number of options are available (International Standards Authority, 1998), but three allocation approaches were assessed for this work: (i) no allocation (the milk takes the entire GHG emission), (ii) mass allocation (based on the mass of product leaving the system annually including surplus calves, culled COWS and 24 month male animals versus the mass of milk) and (iii) economic allocation

based on average milk price 1997-2001 (CSO, 2003) and meat price including subsi- dies (Heavey et al., 1998; Burke and Roche, 1999, 2000, 2001; Connolly et al., 2002). At present there are insufficient Irish data to attempt a system expansion (Weidema, 2001) response to the allocation issue.
18: <header>2.6. Scenarios for GHG emission reduction </header>

In Ireland there are three main reduction scenarios being proposed to address dairy GHG emissions: (i) improved output from COWS requiring fewer animals to supply the national milk quota (O'Donovan, 2000); (ii) a "slaughter scheme" to re- move as many non-milk producing animals from the system as possible (Govern- ment of Ireland, 2000); and (iii) integration of scenarios 1 and 2. These scenarios were tested using the methodology defined for the average dairy unit. The minimum sustainable fertiliser inputs used for each scenario were estimated by system simula- tion (Fitzgerald et al., 2003). The balance of fertiliser to stocking rate and feed de- mand could however occupy a broad range of values and SO each scenario was also evaluated assuming no change in fertiliser rate (i.e., all scenarios used 175 kgNha-1 to define a maximum possible rate. Ranges of diesel consumption figures were also used because there is likely to be reduced machine activity in the systems defined for the scenarios, particularly scenario 2 where there is 35% less stock mean- ing that less slurry will be produced leading to less spreading activity and a lower silage requirement (Rice, 2003). It was calculated that change in the milk protein (0.1-0.3%) that might occur due to higher-genetic-merit COWS (Buckley et al., 2000) would have less than 0.2% influence on the ECM value of a litre of milk and SO this change was ignored. It was assumed that the national milk quota remains the same. The scenarios tested were all assumed to require the same land area as the average system, which implies extensification because there are fewer animals per unit area. This is consistent with the intent of EU policy (Teagasc, 2003).

Scenario 1: "Improved output per cow". The output per cow was raised to 6000 L per 290 day lactation. This scenario places higher-genetic-merit COWS into the system and is in accordance with more efficient milk production in Ireland (O'Donovan, 2000). Increased efficiency reduces the number of COWS to 38. Other stock were ad- justed by assuming a replacement rate of 16%. Artificial fertiliser was reduced to a minimum of 132 Nha Concentrate feed per cow was unchanged because of greater availability of grazed grass from less stock required and COWS with higher ge- netic merit (yielding greater potential to produce more milk). Current recommenda- tions (O'Donovan, 2000) suggest that concentrate use could be reduced to 500 kg, but this was not applied because the nitrogen application rate was reduced in the sce- nario. Diesel usage was estimated to be reduced by 10% as the minimum and the average dairy unit consumption as the maximum. A 10% reduction in fuel could be anticipated given the 15% reduction in stock and 25% reduction in fertiliser used.

Scenario 2: "Slaughter scheme". The number and output of milk-producing cows were assumed to be the same as the defined average. The only other stock held within the system are replacements for the dairy herd at a rate of 16%. The reduced stock

was balanced by a minimum rate of fertiliser application of 122.5 kg Nha-1 Concen- trate feed per cow was unchanged. Diesel usage was estimated to be reduced by 30% as the minimum and the average dairy unit consumption as the maximum. A 30% reduction in fuel use could be justified given the 35% reduction in stock numbers and the 30% reduction in silage required.
Scenario 3: "Integration". A combination of scenarios 1 and 2. Minimum ferti- liser rate was 127 kg Nha-1, and diesel usage was set as scenario 1.

The balance of fertiliser to stocking rate and feed de- mand could however occupy a broad range of values and SO each scenario was also evaluated assuming no change in fertiliser rate (i.e., all scenarios used 175 kgNha-1 to define a maximum possible rate. Ranges of diesel consumption figures were also used because there is likely to be reduced machine activity in the systems defined for the scenarios, particularly scenario 2 where there is 35% less stock mean- ing that less slurry will be produced leading to less spreading activity and a lower silage requirement (Rice, 2003). It was calculated that change in the milk protein (0.1-0.3%) that might occur due to higher-genetic-merit COWS (Buckley et al., 2000) would have less than 0.2% influence on the ECM value of a litre of milk and SO this change was ignored. It was assumed that the national milk quota remains the same. The scenarios tested were all assumed to require the same land area as the average system, which implies extensification because there are fewer animals per unit area. This is consistent with the intent of EU policy (Teagasc, 2003).

Scenario 1: "Improved output per cow". The output per cow was raised to 6000 L per 290 day lactation. This scenario places higher-genetic-merit COWS into the system and is in accordance with more efficient milk production in Ireland (O'Donovan, 2000). Increased efficiency reduces the number of COWS to 38. Other stock were ad- justed by assuming a replacement rate of 16%. Artificial fertiliser was reduced to a minimum of 132 Nha Concentrate feed per cow was unchanged because of greater availability of grazed grass from less stock required and COWS with higher ge- netic merit (yielding greater potential to produce more milk). Current recommenda- tions (O'Donovan, 2000) suggest that concentrate use could be reduced to 500 kg, but this was not applied because the nitrogen application rate was reduced in the sce- nario. Diesel usage was estimated to be reduced by 10% as the minimum and the average dairy unit consumption as the maximum. A 10% reduction in fuel could be anticipated given the 15% reduction in stock and 25% reduction in fertiliser used.

Scenario 2: "Slaughter scheme". The number and output of milk-producing cows were assumed to be the same as the defined average. The only other stock held within the system are replacements for the dairy herd at a rate of 16%. The reduced stock

was balanced by a minimum rate of fertiliser application of 122.5 kg Nha-1 Concen- trate feed per cow was unchanged. Diesel usage was estimated to be reduced by 30% as the minimum and the average dairy unit consumption as the maximum. A 30% reduction in fuel use could be justified given the 35% reduction in stock numbers and the 30% reduction in silage required.
Scenario 3: "Integration". A combination of scenarios 1 and 2. Minimum ferti- liser rate was 127 kg Nha-1, and diesel usage was set as scenario 1.

In Ireland there are three main reduction scenarios being proposed to address dairy GHG emissions: (i) improved output from COWS requiring fewer animals to supply the national milk quota (O'Donovan, 2000); (ii) a "slaughter scheme" to re- move as many non-milk producing animals from the system as possible (Govern- ment of Ireland, 2000); and (iii) integration of scenarios 1 and 2. 21: <header>3.1. The average milk producing unit in Ireland </header>

Irish dairy production was summarised by an average dairy unit comprising 47 milking COWS producing 4822 L per cow per 290 days of lactation (equivalent to Lyr-1). The COWS are fed on grazed grass from mid-March to late October (housed at night at the extremes of the ~200 day grazing season) and supplementation is achieved with silage and concentrates (Table 1) up to 819 kgcowyr-1 supplied when necessary. Cows are milked for 3 h per day, SO for 12.5% of the time they are in the yard during the outdoor grazing period. This means 193 days are allo- cated to grazing and 172 days to housing requiring slurry and manure storage. A period of 870 h per lactation (year) is spent in the collecting yard, and all COWS are dried-off together.

Grazed grass is maintained by the application of nitrogen fertiliser at a rate of 175 kgha-1 and a 100% recycling of organic waste (slurry and manure) is assumed. Grass silage is grown by the unit as conserved forage for the winter period.

Other stock in the unit include 2 non-lactating cows, 28 calves <6 months, 19 male and 7 female 1 to 2 year olds, 1 male and 1 female >2 year old, 1 bull and 7 in calf heifers. The entire non-milk-producing stock is 66 animals. Surplus calves were as- sumed to be sold at 2 weeks and included 8 female and 3 male calves. It is assumed that non-milk-producing stock are kept for at least one year, and graze grass for 178 days except for calves, which graze from day 151 to 304 of the year. All non-milk producing stock are housed on slats for a winter period of 186 days when they are fed with grass silage.

25: <header>3.3. Allocation </header>

Mass allocation indicated 96.6% to milk and 3.4% to meat based on the weight at sale of surplus calves, culled COWS and 24 month male animals. Economic allocation indicated 85% to milk and 15% to meat. Economic allocation has been used by Ced- erberg and Mattsson (2000); Cederberg and Dalerius (2001). Systems expansion might have a more marked influence as found by Cederberg and Stadig (2002), but insufficient data exist to attempt this approach. Similar allocation procedures were used for scenarios evaluation (Table 4).
3.4. Analysis of emissions associated with the average dairy unit

Assuming no allocation, the total GHG emission per kg (ECM) produced in one year, in Ireland was 1.50 kg CO2 eq. The main contributors were: enteric fer- mentation (49%), fertiliser (22%), concentrates (13%), dung management (11%) and diesel/electricity (5%). The result is significantly greater than the 1.03 kg CO2 eq per L milk estimated by Howden and Reyenga (1999) for a dairy unit

Values aggregated by De Boer (2003) indicate a contribution of 48-65% from CH4 from a number of studies on organic and conventional milk production systems in Germany, Sweden and the Netherlands (Haas et al., 2001; Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000 and Iepema and Pijnenburg, 2001). (2001) presented an emission of 1.3 t CO2 per t milk for intensive dairy units in the Allgau region of Germany. The Irish system, when compared with a similar FU, was 1.46 kg CO2 eq (Table 3) to the farm gate. The difference can be attributed to higher output per animal in the German system and differences in management practices at farm level. Ced- erberg and Mattsson (2000) state that the most important contributor to global warming in milk production is methane from enteric fermentation, which is the situation in Ireland.

1: The IPCC default emission factor (EF) of 100 kg CH4 per cow per year was used which represents a western European cow producing an average of 4200 L of milk per year (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 1996b). This value is being evaluated by research in Ireland (Lovett and O'Mara, 2003), but until a definitive re- sult is obtained, it is the best available. Other stock were allocated 50 kg CH4 per animal per year (Environmental Protection Agency, 1998). The IPCC default is 48 kgyr (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 1996b) but recent work in Ire- land suggests a value of 55 kgyr- for equivalent animals in the national beef herd (Lovett and O'Mara, 2003). These EFs merit further investigation because of the dif- ferences that exist within the age profile of the animals that could have a significant effect on the national inventory. A 10% reduction in enteric fermentation from all stock would cause a 5% reduction in kg CO2 eq ECM as calculated by the methodol- ogy presented here.
2: <header>3.6. Analysis of emissions from manure storage and spreading </header>

The manure management assumed was valid because COWS are housed only in win- ter and slurry application occurs early in the grazing season to coincide with growth demand (Carton and Magette, 1999). The Chadwick et al. (1999) EF (0.3% N emitted as N2O, Table 2) was used because it represented the average of a number of different soil types rather than being site-specific. A greater value of 0.725% N (Kroeze, 1994) would have given rise to a 1.2% increase in the GWP of the system. The UK data were considered to be most relevant to the Irish situation. N2O emission (0.5%) from slurry storage was much lower than CH4 (2.5%) relative to the overall GWP of the system.

Site-specific spreading conditions and timing are critical to the actual emissions that can be expected for an individual farm. N2O emissions from April spreading may be 10 times greater than July spreading (Chadwick et al., 2000), but prolonged storage over the summer period would offset any advantage from late spreading. Furthermore nutrients would not be available for grass production SO more mineral fertiliser would be required to meet animal feed demands. The results suggest that emissions from manure management with respect to storage and spreading consti- tuted nearly 4% of the GWP of the system. They should not be ignored during assessment of GWP management options because they can amplify or attenuate ef- fects with respect to animal numbers.
3: <header>3.6. Analysis of emissions from manure storage and spreading </header>

The manure management assumed was valid because COWS are housed only in win- ter and slurry application occurs early in the grazing season to coincide with growth demand (Carton and Magette, 1999). The Chadwick et al. (1999) EF (0.3% N emitted as N2O, Table 2) was used because it represented the average of a number of different soil types rather than being site-specific. A greater value of 0.725% N (Kroeze, 1994) would have given rise to a 1.2% increase in the GWP of the system. The UK data were considered to be most relevant to the Irish situation. N2O emission (0.5%) from slurry storage was much lower than CH4 (2.5%) relative to the overall GWP of the system.

4: <header>3.7. Analysis of emissions from manure excreted at pasture and in the collecting yard </header>

A range of N2O emissions has been suggested (Oenema et al., 1997; Anger et al., 2003; Yamulki et al., 1998) of 0.5-3.0% of total excreted N. N2O emissions that are livestock-induced at pasture are related to the stage of growth of the grass and the time of year, and SO these emissions are variable. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (1996b) default value of 2% was used, which suggested that N2O

from dung let down at pasture contributed 7% of the GWP of the system. A 10% reduction in N excretion at grazing would cause a 1.3% change in kg CO2 eq. Reduc- tions in N excretion from dietary manipulation with low degradable protein and high-starch feed could give greater reductions (Kebreab et al., 2001). Methane from dung in the field constituted ~0.1% to the GWP of the system, which is insignificant and can be ignored. Yard emissions are SO small that the 0.0002% contribution can safely be ignored in future.
5: <header>3.8. Analysis of emissions from concentrate feed production </header>

The most important feature of Table 1 is the wide variety of ingredients used to make up the concentrate feed with particular reference to their origins (60% from outside Ireland). The GWP of concentrates was 13% of the total GWP of the system. The transportation and processing of the products only accounted for 9% with 91% of the emissions associated with the primary production of ingredients. If the amount of concentrate feed per cow were reduced by 30%, there would be a 4% reduction in GWP. This option would be feasible given that advisory recommenda- tions for concentrate feed on a grass-based system are 54% lower than the average actually used (Donnellan et al., 2002). A replacement of ingredients such as rapeseed, which has a very high GWP, with ingredients of equal nutritional value but lower GWP, could significantly reduce emissions. Perhaps the ideal, from the point-of-view of total emissions from the system, would be the introduction of a home mixed, unprocessed ration of locally-sourced products. The use of food by-products such as unsatisfactory whole potatoes from the crisping industry, or brewer's grains from the brewing industry would be suitable feeds, especially because potatoes have a CO2 equivalent emission 72% lower than barley and 91% lower than Soya beans (Nielsen et al., 2003).
6: <header>3.9. Analysis of emissions from artificial fertiliser production and field application </header>

The default value of 1.25% of the N applied (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 1996b) was used as the N2O EF because of the lack of Irish-specific data. Background emissions of CH4, N2O and CO2 from soil were not considered. Prelim- inary results from Irish work (not published) suggest the value of 1.25% may under- estimate the actual contribution (Hyde et al., 2004). Site-specific descriptors (Scanlon and Kiely, 2003; Bouwman et al., 2002) may prove necessary to derive regionally weighted average emissions, but at present the best estimate is the default value. The production of artificial fertiliser is associated with substantial emissions of CH4, N2O and CO2, the main contributors of which are the fossil fuel input required for production and N2O from nitric acid (Kaltschmitt and Reinhardt, 1997). The production and transportation of fertiliser contributed nearly 14% of the total GWP of the system and the subsequent application of fertiliser added an additional 8%. Excluding dung in the field (which is always part of the system) there is three times more CO2 (equivalent) emitted per kilogram of mineral fertiliser N than organic N used by an Irish dairy unit. An optimum N management strategy should be
devised to consider environmental impacts such as GHG emissions, not just yield and N leaching as is currently the case in Ireland.

8: <header>3.10. Analysis of emissions from diesel and electricity consumption </header>

Total diesel usage contributed 5% of the GWP of the system. There is, however, relatively little that can be done within the average system to reduce the amount of diesel consumed. Extensification should result in less fertiliser and slurry application and less grass to ensile, as estimated for the scenarios considered.

Ireland has a wide variety of modes of electricity generation ranging from wind to oil. An EF for CO2 per kWh produced was extrapolated to cover all modes of gen- eration (Howley et al., 2002). Electricity usage on farms contributed a very small amount of the GWP of the system and its reduction is not very likely with changes in intensity or other management practice unless a more energy efficient means of milk preservation is found.
9: GWP from enteric fermentation is the most important contributor to the system. Reduction scenarios should be assessed for shift of emissions from one part of the emissions basket to another and also for amplification/attenuation effects by lesser components within the system. For example, the digestibility of the diet to decrease the enteric fermentation emission from the cow needs to be considered in light of the 13% contribution by concentrate feed, and whether a small change in enteric fermen- tation would be off-set by an increase in the emissions due to concentrate production. There are insufficient data available in Ireland regarding feed options to test fully a feed option scenario at present, but the scenarios currently being considered were evaluated (Table 4) with respect to changes in kg CO2 eq and the amplifiers and attenuators.

Scenario 1: Martin and Seeland (1999) observed that a more intensive dairy unit would decrease the methane production per litre of milk produced. This scenario suggests a similar trend. Increasing cow efficiency seems the best method for reduc- ing emissions because by increasing output per cow the total number of COWS in Ire- land will be reduced. The results indicate a reduction in kg CO2 eqecm to 1.23 (Table 4) with minimum fertilizer and diesel. The reduction in cow numbers causes a 9.5% reduction from enteric fermentation, but there is an estimated amplifying effect of a further 13% reduction due to the complementary nutrient management and concen- trate feed changes. Even if a higher default value of 118 kgCH4yr~ (Intergovern- mental Panel on Climate Change, 1996b) per cow for higher output animals were adopted, there would still be a 14% reduction from the average kg CO2

Scenario 2: Implementation of a "slaughter scheme" where non-milk-producing stock are minimised causes a reduction in kg CO2 eqecm to 1.11 (Table 4) with min- imum fertilizer and diesel. This is mainly due to a combination of reduced stock numbers (20% reduction in kg CO2 eqecm) and reduced inputs (15% reduction in kg CO2 eqecm). Adopting the "slaughter scheme" nationally would be consistent

with the steady increase in suckler cow numbers since the mid 1980s in Ireland (McCarty et al., 1996). This is largely due to increased specialisation in the dairy sec- tor which has already started decreasing beef supply from dairy units (McCarty et al., 1996). Recent reforms to the Common Agricultural Policy, intended to reduce animal numbers, is a political policy that is also consistent with this scenario (Tea- gasc, 2003). This means that specialisation in milk production in an Irish context should reduce GHG emissions from the agricultural sector as a whole without undue pressure on any one sub-sector. The amplifying effect beyond animal numbers is less evident for this scenario because there is little change in concentrate consumption.

Scenario 3: The application of the "integrated" scenario yielded the lowest kg CO2 eqecm of 0.98 (Table 4) with minimum fertilizer and diesel. This leads to a 31% reduction from enteric fermentation and 22% from changes in the amount of concen- trate fed, fertiliser consumed, and manure produced.

Applying the higher rate of fertiliser (175 Nha-1 and diesel consumption (4527 kgyr-1 to all the scenarios gave a reduction in kg CO2 eqecm between 16% and 30% (Table 4) less than the average dairy unit currently operating in Ireland (Ta- ble 3). However use of fertiliser N at this higher rate is unlikely because it would lead to excess N within the system and is incompatible with environmental legislation.

Allocation had relatively little effect on the scenario results because each scenario was designed to reduce outputs (mass and economic) that were not milk from the system. This means that the system is effectively more efficient at producing its pri- mary product for each scenario.

13: <header>4. Conclusions </header>

Analysis should be carried out on a whole system basis to identify legitimate reduction strategies. The approach taken in this work should be considered for the evaluation of any proposed changes to an agricultural production system because it allows the assessment of individual strategies for GHG reduction and does not fo- cus on one particular element. Analysis of the results indicates that having consid- ered the larger contributors to kg CO2 eqecm, it is important to examine the smaller ones for amplifying and attenuating effects. These include concentrate feed, manure management and diesel consumption. A number of issues emerged for gen- eral application to livestock systems that would not be apparent if only the larger emission sources were considered. Other important issues that arise (and are appli- cable to both emission accounting type inventories and LCA type studies) which need to be considered in further work are: (i) the necessity to properly quantify the age profile and EF of all animals in more detail than is afforded by using just two categories i.e., dairy COWS and all other cattle; (ii) the nature of manure and the timing of spreading because the results suggest that storage of manure contrib- uted 3% of the total GWP while spreading was <1%; (iii) the amount of N excreted, because N2O from dung let down at pasture contributed nearly 2% of the GWP of the system; (iv) the application of fertiliser at 8% of the GWP has the potential to be further reduced; (v) the role of concentrate ingredients and sources, which could sig-

nificantly reduce the GHG emissions; and (vi) the role of extensification versus inten- sification and the related influence on N and concentrate demand of the system. The future for Irish milk production with Common Agricultural Policy decou- pling being implemented should lead to a reduction in cow numbers, and increased specialisation in the sector is driving farmers to use higher-output cows. The results from this work indicate that these trends in Irish dairy production will lead to a reduction in the GHG emissions by the sector.
